title: Understanding Brittle and Splitting Dog Nails: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's nails are splitting, when to see a vet (Low Urgency), and how photos help with diagnosis and home care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Brittle or splitting nails in dogs are usually non-emergencies unless accompanied by uncontrollable bleeding or severe pain. You should schedule a routine vet visit to identify underlying causes and protect the sensitive nail quick from further injury.

What are brittle or splitting nails in dogs and what causes them?

Brittle or splitting nails, medically known as onychodystrophy, occur when a dog's claws become dry, weak, and prone to cracking or peeling in layers. This condition can affect a single nail due to trauma or multiple nails due to underlying health issues. When a nail splits, it can expose the sensitive quick—the living tissue inside the nail containing nerves and blood vessels—which can be quite painful for your pet.

How do I know if my dog's split nail is a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for brittle or splitting nails is generally low, as it is rarely a life-threatening emergency.
  • Schedule a routine veterinary appointment to investigate the cause and manage your dog's discomfort.
  • Contact a clinic for immediate advice if there is profuse bleeding that does not stop with pressure.
  • Seek care sooner if your dog is showing signs of extreme pain or is refusing to walk.

How can a photo of my dog's nails help a veterinarian with triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows the vet to see the specific pattern of the split.
  • Visuals help the team check for signs of infection at the nail bed.
  • A photo can help assess whether the sensitive quick is exposed or damaged.
  • This information helps triage staff determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen.
  • Photos provide a baseline for monitoring the condition and ensuring the nail heals properly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Horizontal fissures in dog nails result from disrupted horn production at the dermis beneath the coronary band, creating defects parallel to the coronary band. Severity ranges from shallow grooves to complete wall fractures. These fissures may be associated with stress, such as post-weaning or nutritional deprivation, and move distally as the claw grows, potentially leading to a fractured distal portion ("broken toe"). Multiple grooves can weaken the dorsal wall, causing it to bend. Examination includes physical assessment and radiographs to evaluate hoof wall separation and distal phalanx displacement.

Chapter: Surgery, Podiatry

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1085)

faqs.question: What common conditions cause brittle nails?
faqs.answer: Common causes include nutritional deficiencies (such as a lack of omega-3 fatty acids), fungal infections, or immune-mediated diseases like Symmetrical Lupoid Onychodystrophy (SLO).

faqs.question: Can I trim a split nail myself?
faqs.answer: If the nail is dangling or split far from the quick, you may be able to carefully trim the loose piece. However, if the split extends into the quick, it is best to let a veterinarian handle it to prevent pain and further injury.

faqs.question: How can I strengthen my dog's nails?
faqs.answer: Consult your vet about adding biotin or fish oil supplements to your dog's diet, and ensure their nails are trimmed regularly to prevent snags and trauma.
